concepts.lesson: A market is a place where buyers and sellers meet to exchange goods and services. Markets can be physical places such as shops, streets, or malls, and they help people buy what they need for daily life.
concepts.real_example: A vegetable market in a town where farmers and traders sell fruits and vegetables to customers.
key_points: Place where buying and selling happens
Essential for daily goods and services

concepts.lesson: Weekly markets are markets that take place once a week in a specific area. Traders bring goods such as vegetables, clothes, utensils, and household items and sell them to local people.
concepts.real_example: A Sunday market where traders set up temporary stalls to sell fruits, clothes, and kitchen items.
key_points: Goods usually sold at lower prices

concepts.lesson: Neighbourhood markets consist of shops located close to residential areas. These shops sell daily necessities such as groceries, milk, bread, and vegetables.
concepts.real_example: A local grocery shop where people buy rice, oil, and sugar for daily use.

concepts.lesson: Shopping complexes and malls are large buildings with many shops selling branded goods, clothes, electronics, and other products. They usually have fixed prices and modern facilities.
concepts.real_example: A large shopping mall in a city where people buy branded clothes, shoes, and gadgets.

concepts.lesson: Retailers are sellers who sell goods directly to consumers in small quantities. They purchase goods from wholesalers and make them available in local shops.
concepts.real_example: A shopkeeper who buys biscuits, soaps, and snacks from wholesalers and sells them in a grocery shop.

concepts.lesson: Wholesalers buy goods in large quantities directly from producers and sell them to retailers. They play an important role in distributing goods across markets.
concepts.real_example: A wholesale vegetable trader buying produce from farmers and supplying it to many retail vegetable vendors.

concepts.lesson: The chain of markets refers to the series of steps through which goods pass from producers to consumers. Goods usually move from producers to wholesalers, then to retailers, and finally to buyers.
concepts.real_example: Farmers sell vegetables to wholesalers, who then supply retailers in local markets where consumers buy them.

concepts.lesson: Prices of goods can vary in different markets because of transportation costs, shop rent, storage expenses, and seller profit margins.
concepts.real_example: Vegetables may be cheaper in a weekly market but cost more in an air-conditioned shopping mall.
key_points: Prices depend on costs and demand

concepts.concept_name: Role of Markets in Daily Life
concepts.lesson: Markets play an important role in everyday life by making goods and services available to people. They allow producers to sell their products and consumers to buy what they need.
concepts.real_example: People visit markets to buy vegetables, clothes, school supplies, and household items.

concepts.lesson: Competition among sellers encourages better quality products and reasonable prices. When many sellers offer similar goods, consumers have more choices.
concepts.real_example: Several clothing shops selling similar shirts may lower prices or offer discounts to attract customers.
